---
title: "Text Analysis GenCom: Generating Code and Analysis"
output: html_document
date: "2026-07-06"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

This is a note pad for the data preparation and initial analysis of the General Comments Paper (Network and Diskurs Analysis).

# Transform Documents
The first step is to transform and merge the data. I exported the text data from two MAXQDA files that contain the network coding and all the available texts. MAXQDA only allows exports in Excel, which -- however -- only allows up to ca. 32.000 characters per line. Although the MAXQDA export orinigally stores the text in several lines, the text analysis requires text in one line to generate a comprehensive data set. 
Thus, the transformation needs to transform the text first into CSV and than generate the data set.



## Prepare

```         
install.packages("readxl")

setwd("D:/Research/Web of Human Rights/GenComs/text")

```

## Transforming Excel into CSV
Excel files are stored in one separet folder. CSV files go into a new folder for all texts.

```
library(readxl)

# Files with raw data and output
input_dir  <- "all_xlsx"
output_dir <- "all"

# List all xlsx-Dat in input-file
files <- list.files(input_dir, pattern = "\\.xlsx$", full.names = TRUE)

# Loop: read files and save as new CSV in new folder
for (f in files) {
  df <- read_excel(f)
  
  out_name <- paste0(tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(f)), ".csv")
  out_path <- file.path(output_dir, out_name)
  
  write.csv(df, out_path, row.names = FALSE, fileEncoding = "UTF-8")
}

```

## Batching over all docs
This needs to run over all GenCom Docs now in CSV format.

```         
library(dplyr)

files <- list.files("all", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
docs_list <- lapply(files, read.csv, stringsAsFactors = FALSE, encoding = "UTF-8")
names(docs_list) <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(files))

collapse_doc <- function(df, text_col = 2) {
  paste(df[[text_col]], collapse = "\n\n")
}

collapsed_all <- sapply(docs_list, collapse_doc)


dataset <- data.frame(
  doc_id = names(collapsed_all),
  text   = collapsed_all,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
```

## Exporting Data set

```         
#write text
writeLines(dataset$text, "datatext_all.txt")

#export
write.csv2(dataset, "dataset_all.csv", row.names = FALSE)




```
## Emerging Problems

Biggist issue / bug: Starting with UN1997GenRecCEDAW23 in row 366 or line 386 in the original file, the text "leaves" the row in the data set and starts a new row in the aggregate data set here: "10 See paragraph 141 // of the Plaform of action..". This happens several time in the data set generating process, and I am not sure what to do about it. Initially, I though the text reaches Excel's character limit in one row (ca. 32.000), but it also happens in CSV. Since the break-up is arbitraty, there must be something in the treatment of the text. The text itself does not seem to have a break.



## To Do
 (Keep titles) (?)
 
 
### Analysis

clustering
sementic distribution
readability
supervised classification


- how is the discursive authority organized, is there a textual dimension to explain the network position

RQ: Does the text explain the network position? 


- specific framing of a new HR interpretation: VaW Gen Com on shifting human rights interpretation, --> look for examples that represent the textual/discursive linkages that you expect
non-technical references --> classification or extraction , name entry recognition task
- What are text level co-variance that make the text (in terms of theory network) central?

- What about GenComs jointly produced by Committees?

+ contructing the Data set
++ Overview Data
- doc_id, text, indegree, outdegree, betweenness, 
- 
++ Dyadic Network Data
- basic doc_id dyadic data

- Identify examples to grasp what these textual representation might actualyl look like.

+ Analysis: supervised extraction, undersupervised extraction








## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```         
```

# Appendix
This includes old ideas and residual code.

## First idea Read texts

```         
# Read data
UN2017GenComCAT4 <- read_excel("UN2017GenComCAT4.xlsx")

# Collapse into a single string, paragraphs separated by double newline
doc_text <- paste(UN2017GenComCAT4[[2]], collapse = "\n\n")

# If you want it as a one-row tibble preserving the structure:
UN2017GenComCAT4_collapsed <- tibble(
  doc_id = 1,
  committee = "Committee against Torture",
  text = paste(UN2017GenComCAT4 [[2]], collapse = "\n\n")
)

#if you need to batch this across documents than I would do soemthing like
this
collapse_doc <- function(df, text_col = 2) {
  paste(df[[text_col]], collapse = "\n\n")
}
```
## Old bathcing code
```
library(readxl)
library(dplyr)

files <- list.files("all", pattern = "\\.xlsx$", full.names = TRUE)
docs_list <- lapply(files, read_excel)
names(docs_list) <- tools::file_path_sans_ext(basename(files))

collapse_doc <- function(df, text_col = 2) {
  paste(df[[text_col]], collapse = "\n\n")
}

collapsed_all <- sapply(docs_list, collapse_doc)


dataset <- data.frame(
  doc_id = names(collapsed_all),
  text   = collapsed_all,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

#Check
lengths <- sapply(docs_list, function(df) nchar(df[[2]]))
lengths  # oder: 
which(lengths > 32000)


```


\*\*Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.



library(openxlsx)
setwd("D:/Research/Web of Human Rights/GenComs/wd")

# CSV einlesen
df <- read.csv("data_gc.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Neues Workbook erstellen
wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "Daten")

# Daten schreiben
writeData(wb, "Daten", df)

# Spaltenbreite automatisch anpassen
setColWidths(wb, "Daten", cols = 1:ncol(df), widths = "auto")

# Kopfzeile formatieren (fett, Hintergrundfarbe)
headerStyle <- createStyle(textDecoration = "bold", fgFill = "#D9E1F2", 
                            halign = "center", border = "Bottom")
addStyle(wb, "Daten", headerStyle, rows = 1, cols = 1:ncol(df))

# Filter auf Kopfzeile aktivieren
freezePane(wb, "Daten", firstRow = TRUE)

# Speichern
saveWorkbook(wb, "dataset_formatiert.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
